**Ozaukee County** is a [county](/wiki/County_(United_States) "County (United States)") in the [U.S. state](/wiki/U.S._state "U.S. state") of [Wisconsin](/wiki/Wisconsin "Wisconsin"). As of the [2020 census](/wiki/2020_United_States_census "2020 United States census"), the population was 91,503.[[1]](#cite_note-2020-census-55089-1) Its [county seat](/wiki/County_seat "County seat") is [Port Washington](/wiki/Port_Washington,_Wisconsin "Port Washington, Wisconsin").[[2]](#cite_note-GR6-2) Ozaukee County is included in the [Milwaukee metropolitan area](/wiki/Milwaukee_metropolitan_area "Milwaukee metropolitan area").

As of the 2000 Census, Ozaukee County had the second-lowest [poverty rate](/wiki/Poverty_rate "Poverty rate") of any county in the United States, at 2.6%. In terms of per capita income, it is the 25th-wealthiest county in the country.

## Toponymy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ozaukee_County,_Wisconsin&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Toponymy")]

"Ozaukee" comes from the [Ojibwe](/wiki/Ojibwe_language "Ojibwe language") name for the Sauk people. It probably means "people living at the mouth of a river."[[3]](#cite_note-whs-3)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ozaukee_County,_Wisconsin&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

### Precolonial

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ozaukee_County,_Wisconsin&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Precolonial")]

The Hilgen Spring Mound Site is one of the oldest-known sites of human habitation of Ozaukee County. Located near [Cedar Creek](/wiki/Cedar_Creek_(Wisconsin) "Cedar Creek (Wisconsin)") in the eastern part of the City of [Cedarburg](/wiki/Cedarburg,_Wisconsin "Cedarburg, Wisconsin"), the site consists of three conical burial mounds constructed by early [Woodland period](/wiki/Woodland_period "Woodland period") [Mound Builders](/wiki/Mound_Builders "Mound Builders").[[4]](#cite_note-4) In 1968, archaeologists from the [Milwaukee Public Museum](/wiki/Milwaukee_Public_Museum "Milwaukee Public Museum") found human burials and artifacts, including stone altars, arrowheads, and pottery shards, during an excavation of one of the mounds. [Radiocarbon samples](/wiki/Radiocarbon_dating "Radiocarbon dating") from the excavation date the mounds' construction to approximately 480 BCE, making it one of the oldest mound groups in the state.[[5]](#cite_note-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6)

In the mid-1800s, [Increase A. Lapham](/wiki/Increase_A._Lapham "Increase A. Lapham") identified a group of circular mounds in the [Saukville](/wiki/Saukville,_Wisconsin "Saukville, Wisconsin") area and found a stone ax. In his writing, Lapham did not speculate about the age of the artifact or the mounds.[[7]](#cite_note-7) An additional artifact of the early Native American presence in the Saukville area is the Ozaukee County [Birdstone](/wiki/Bird_stone "Bird stone"), discovered by a six-year-old farm boy in 1891.[[8]](#cite_note-8) While the exact age of the Ozaukee County Birdstone remains uncertain, many birdstones date from a period ranging from 3000 BCE to 500 BCE.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

### 19th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ozaukee_County,_Wisconsin&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 19th century")]

In the early 19th century, the Native Americans living in Ozaukee County included the [Menominee](/wiki/Menominee "Menominee"), [Potawatomi](/wiki/Potawatomi "Potawatomi"), and [Sauk people](/wiki/Sauk_people "Sauk people"). There were numerous Native American villages in the county along the [Milwaukee River](/wiki/Milwaukee_River "Milwaukee River") and its tributaries. The Menominee surrendered their claims to the land east of the Milwaukee River to the United States Federal Government in 1832 through the [Treaty of Washington](/wiki/Treaty_of_Washington,_with_Menominee_(1831) "Treaty of Washington, with Menominee (1831)"). The Potawatomi surrendered their claims to the land west of the river in 1833 through the [1833 Treaty of Chicago](/wiki/1833_Treaty_of_Chicago "1833 Treaty of Chicago"), which (after being ratified in 1835) required them to leave the area by 1838. While many Potawatomi people moved west of the Mississippi River to [Kansas](/wiki/Kansas "Kansas"), some chose to remain in Wisconsin, and were known as "strolling Potawatomi" because they were migrant [squatters](/wiki/Squatting "Squatting"). Eventually the Potawatomi who evaded forced removal gathered in northern Wisconsin, where they formed the [Forest County Potawatomi Community](/wiki/Forest_County_Potawatomi_Community "Forest County Potawatomi Community").[[10]](#cite_note-10)[[11]](#cite_note-11)

The first whites in the area were primarily New England land speculators, who began purchasing land from the government in 1835 at the price of $1.25 per acre.[[12]](#cite_note-12) One of these land speculators was Wooster Harrison, who settled the land that would become [Port Washington](/wiki/Port_Washington,_Wisconsin "Port Washington, Wisconsin") in 1835, which he originally named "Wisconsin City."[[13]](#cite_note-price-13)[[14]](#cite_note-14) At the time, the land was part of [Washington County](/wiki/Washington_County,_Wisconsin "Washington County, Wisconsin"), and there were proposals that Port Washington become the county seat.[[15]](#cite_note-ReferenceA-15) However, Port Washington was far from the county's other early settlements, including [Mequon](/wiki/Mequon,_Wisconsin "Mequon, Wisconsin"), [Grafton](/wiki/Grafton_(town),_Wisconsin "Grafton (town), Wisconsin") and [Germantown](/wiki/Germantown,_Wisconsin "Germantown, Wisconsin"). In 1850, the Wisconsin legislature bisected Washington County into northern and southern counties, with Port Washington as the northern seat and [Cedarburg](/wiki/Cedarburg,_Wisconsin "Cedarburg, Wisconsin") as the southern. County residents failed to ratify the bill, and in 1853 the legislature instead bisected the county into eastern and western sections, creating Ozaukee County. Port Washington became the seat of the new county, and the Washington County seat moved to [West Bend](/wiki/West_Bend,_Wisconsin "West Bend, Wisconsin").[[16]](#cite_note-16)

In the 1840s, [German](/wiki/Germans "Germans"), [Irish](/wiki/Irish_people "Irish people"), and [Luxembourger](/wiki/Luxembourgers "Luxembourgers") immigrants began settling in the county. Germans were the largest ethnic group in and 19th century Ozaukee County,[[17]](#cite_note-ReferenceB-17) with seven in eight residents being of German descent according to the 1870 census.[[18]](#cite_note-18) The earliest settlements formed around grist- and sawmills located on the county's waterways. [Cedarburg](/wiki/Cedarburg,_Wisconsin "Cedarburg, Wisconsin"), [Grafton](/wiki/Grafton,_Wisconsin "Grafton, Wisconsin"), [Hamilton](/wiki/Hamilton,_Ozaukee_County,_Wisconsin "Hamilton, Ozaukee County, Wisconsin"), [Newburg](/wiki/Newburg,_Wisconsin "Newburg, Wisconsin"), [Saukville](/wiki/Saukville,_Wisconsin "Saukville, Wisconsin"), and [Thiensville](/wiki/Thiensville,_Wisconsin "Thiensville, Wisconsin") all had mills by end of the 1840s. In the 19th and early 20th centuries, the county economy was primarily based on agriculture.[[17]](#cite_note-ReferenceB-17)

The beginning of the [American Civil War](/wiki/American_Civil_War "American Civil War") saw some chaos in Ozaukee County. The county was one of the areas affected by Wisconsin's "Great Indian Scare" of September 1862, in which some residents panicked because of unfounded rumors of a Native American uprising in the state. The panic was exacerbated by the fact that 30,000 Wisconsinites were away, serving in the war, so residents may have felt especially vulnerable. Some residents fled their homes for Milwaukee, while others holed up in makeshift fortresses, as happened at the [Cedarburg Mill](/wiki/Cedarburg_Mill "Cedarburg Mill").[[19]](#cite_note-19) Several months after the panic, the United States Congress implemented the draft, which was unpopular among German immigrants with bad memories of mandatory conscription in their homelands.[[20]](#cite_note-20) On November 10, 1862, several hundred Port Washington residents marched on the courthouse, attacked the official in charge of implementing the draft, burned draft records, and vandalized the homes of Union supporters. The riot ended when eight detachments of Union troops from Milwaukee were deployed.[[21]](#cite_note-21)

In the 1870s the [Milwaukee & Northern Railway](/wiki/Milwaukee_and_Northern_Railway_Company "Milwaukee and Northern Railway Company") was constructed to connect Milwaukee and northern Wisconsin including Green Bay,[[22]](#cite_note-:0-22) along its route it reached many communities in the center of the county including Thiensville, Cedarburg, Grafton and Saukville.[[22]](#cite_note-:0-22) Around the same time the [Milwaukee, Lake Shore and Western Railway](/wiki/Milwaukee,_Lake_Shore_and_Western_Railway "Milwaukee, Lake Shore and Western Railway") constructed its railway on the eastern edge of the county along Lake Michigan, also to connect Milwaukee and Northern Wisconsin.[[23]](#cite_note-:1-23) It reached fewer communities compared to the M&N line, only serving Port Washington.[[23]](#cite_note-:1-23) Regardless the railroads spurred development in Ozaukee County by providing efficient freight and passenger transportation.

### 20th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ozaukee_County,_Wisconsin&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 20th century")]

From 1908 to 1940, [the Milwaukee Electric Railway and Light Company](/wiki/The_Milwaukee_Electric_Railway_and_Light_Company "The Milwaukee Electric Railway and Light Company") (TMERL) provided electric [interurban](/wiki/Interurban "Interurban") passenger and freight service from Sheboygan to Milwaukee with stops at Belgium, Port Washington, Grafton, Cedarburg, Thiensville, Mequon, and other villages as well as major road crossings within Ozaukee County. The interurban cars ran approximately once per hour and delivered Ozaukee County agricultural products, such as milk and meat, to Milwaukee grocers and butchers. In 1940, the interurban ceased servicing Sheboygan due to declining ridership. Port Washington became the line's new northern terminus[[24]](#cite_note-24) before the Ozaukee County line ceased operation in 1948.[[17]](#cite_note-ReferenceB-17)

Ozaukee County's communities experienced significant population growth during the [suburbanization](/wiki/Suburbanization "Suburbanization") that followed World War II. Between 1940 and 1980, the population more than tripled, from 18,985 to 66,981. Although the interurban to Milwaukee declined service and finally ceased operation after the war, the construction of [Interstate 43](/wiki/Interstate_43 "Interstate 43") in the mid-1960s allowed more residents to commute long distances to jobs and this encouraged residential home construction.[[17]](#cite_note-ReferenceB-17) Communities that experienced the most significant population growth, such as Cedarburg and Grafton, began to annex agricultural land for residential subdivisions and retail commercial development.[[25]](#cite_note-25) The previously rural Town of Mequon became increasingly suburban and incorporated in 1957 as the City of Mequon. Today, it is the largest and most populous city in Ozaukee County.

### 21st century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ozaukee_County,_Wisconsin&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: 21st century")]

The [Wisconsin Shipwreck Coast National Marine Sanctuary](/wiki/Wisconsin_Shipwreck_Coast_National_Marine_Sanctuary "Wisconsin Shipwreck Coast National Marine Sanctuary") was established in 2021 in the waters of Lake Michigan, with its southern portion lying off roughly the northern half of Ozaukee County′s coastline. The [national marine sanctuary](/wiki/National_Marine_Sanctuary "National Marine Sanctuary") is the site of a large number of historically significant [shipwrecks](/wiki/Shipwreck "Shipwreck").[[26]](#cite_note-26)[[27]](#cite_note-27)[[28]](#cite_note-28)

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ozaukee_County,_Wisconsin&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Geography")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b8/LionsDen_Oz-Co-WI.png/250px-LionsDen_Oz-Co-WI.png)](/wiki/File:LionsDen_Oz-Co-WI.png)

Lake Michigan shoreline, south of [Port Washington](/wiki/Port_Washington,_Wisconsin "Port Washington, Wisconsin")

Ozaukee County covers 233 square miles of land, making it the second smallest county in Wisconsin by land area after [Pepin County](/wiki/Pepin_County,_Wisconsin "Pepin County, Wisconsin"). The county's jurisdiction also extends over 883 square miles of water, most of which is in [Lake Michigan](/wiki/Lake_Michigan "Lake Michigan").[[29]](#cite_note-29)[[30]](#cite_note-30)

[Lion's Den Gorge Nature Preserve](/wiki/Lion%27s_Den_Gorge_Nature_Preserve "Lion's Den Gorge Nature Preserve") is a large bluffland and wetland county protected area on the shore of Lake Michigan.

### 2020 census

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ozaukee_County,_Wisconsin&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: 2020 census")]

As of the [2020 census](/wiki/2020_United_States_census "2020 United States census"), the county had a population of 91,503 and the population density was 392.7 people per square mile (151.6 people/km2).[[37]](#cite_note-Census2020PL-37)[[1]](#cite_note-2020-census-55089-1) There were 39,086 housing units at an average density of 167.7 units per square mile (64.7 units/km2).[[1]](#cite_note-2020-census-55089-1)

The median age was 44.5 years. 21.5% of residents were under the age of 18 and 21.0% of residents were 65 years of age or older. For every 100 females there were 95.4 males, and for every 100 females age 18 and over there were 92.7 males age 18 and over.[[38]](#cite_note-Census2020DP-38)

There were 37,015 households in the county, of which 28.1% had children under the age of 18 living in them. Of all households, 57.9% were married-couple households, 14.6% were households with a male householder and no spouse or partner present, and 22.7% were households with a female householder and no spouse or partner present. About 27.1% of all households were made up of individuals and 13.7% had someone living alone who was 65 years of age or older. Of the 39,086 housing units, 5.3% were vacant, and among occupied units 75.6% were owner-occupied and 24.4% were renter-occupied; the homeowner vacancy rate was 0.8% and the rental vacancy rate was 6.8%.[[38]](#cite_note-Census2020DP-38)

The racial makeup of the county was 89.9% [White](/wiki/White_Americans "White Americans"), 1.7% [Black or African American](/wiki/African_Americans "African Americans"), 0.3% [American Indian and Alaska Native](/wiki/Native_Americans_in_the_United_States "Native Americans in the United States"), 2.5% [Asian](/wiki/Asian_Americans "Asian Americans"), <0.1% [Native Hawaiian](/wiki/Native_Hawaiians "Native Hawaiians") and [Pacific Islander](/wiki/Pacific_Islander "Pacific Islander"), 1.1% from some other race, and 4.6% from [two or more races](/wiki/Multiracial_Americans "Multiracial Americans"). [Hispanic or Latino](/wiki/Hispanic_and_Latino_Americans "Hispanic and Latino Americans") residents of any race comprised 3.4% of the population.[[37]](#cite_note-Census2020PL-37)

75.7% of residents lived in urban areas, while 24.3% lived in rural areas.[[39]](#cite_note-Census2020DHC-39)

### 2000 census

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ozaukee_County,_Wisconsin&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: 2000 census")]

As of the [census](/wiki/Census "Census")[[40]](#cite_note-GR8-40) of 2000, there were 82,317 people, 30,857 households, and 23,019 families residing in the county. The [population density](/wiki/Population_density "Population density") was 355 people per square mile (137 people/km2). There were 32,034 housing units at an average density of 138 units per square mile (53 units/km2). The racial makeup of the county was 96.72% [White](/wiki/Race_(United_States_Census) "Race (United States Census)"), 0.93% [Black](/wiki/Race_(United_States_Census) "Race (United States Census)") or [African American](/wiki/Race_(United_States_Census) "Race (United States Census)"), 0.20% [Native American](/wiki/Race_(United_States_Census) "Race (United States Census)"), 1.07% [Asian](/wiki/Race_(United_States_Census) "Race (United States Census)"), 0.02% [Pacific Islander](/wiki/Race_(United_States_Census) "Race (United States Census)"), 0.34% from [other races](/wiki/Race_(United_States_Census) "Race (United States Census)"), and 0.73% from two or more races. 1.30% of the population were [Hispanic](/wiki/Race_(United_States_Census) "Race (United States Census)") or [Latino](/wiki/Race_(United_States_Census) "Race (United States Census)") of any race. 47.2% were of [German](/wiki/German_people "German people"), 7.3% [Irish](/wiki/Irish_people "Irish people") and 6.7% [Polish](/wiki/Polish_people "Polish people") ancestry. 95.1% spoke [English](/wiki/English_language "English language"), 1.6% [Spanish](/wiki/Spanish_language "Spanish language") and 1.4% [German](/wiki/German_language "German language") as their first language.

There were 30,857 households, out of which 36.00% had children under the age of 18 living with them, 65.60% were [married couples](/wiki/Marriage "Marriage") living together, 6.50% had a female householder with no husband present, and 25.40% were non-families. 21.40% of all households were made up of individuals, and 8.40% had someone living alone who was 65 years of age or older. The average household size was 2.61 and the average family size was 3.07.

In the county, the population was spread out, with 26.60% under the age of 18, 6.80% from 18 to 24, 28.00% from 25 to 44, 25.90% from 45 to 64, and 12.60% who were 65 years of age or older. The median age was 39 years. For every 100 females there were 97.30 males. For every 100 females age 18 and over, there were 94 males.

The median income for a household in the county was $62,745, and the median income for a family was $72,547 (these figures had risen to $73,197 and $88,231 respectively as of a 2007 estimate[[41]](#cite_note-41)). Males had a median income of $50,044 versus $30,476 for females. The per capita income for the county was $31,947. About 1.7% of families and 2.6% of the population were below the poverty line, including 2.6% of those under age 18 and 4.1% of those age 65 or over.

Ozaukee County has a harbor in Port Washington on Lake Michigan, though not in the lakeside communities of Mequon or Grafton due to high bluffs along the lakeshore.

The [Ozaukee County Interurban Trail](/wiki/Ozaukee_Interurban_Trail "Ozaukee Interurban Trail") is a multimodal trail for pedestrians and non-motorized vehicles. It runs through Grafton and connects to Sheboygan County and Brown Deer Trails via the old Milwaukee-Sheboygan Passenger Rail line.

Public transit was formerly provided by a commuter express bus (Route 143) to Milwaukee with stops in Port Washington, Saukville, Grafton, and Mequon. The bus operated Monday through Fridays excluding holidays, and was run jointly by Milwaukee and Ozaukee County. In 2024 the Ozaukee County board elected to discontinue the transit line to Milwaukee without replacement leaving Ozaukee County with no public transit connection to Milwaukee and no fixed route transit service within the county. The last day of service was June 28, 2024.[[44]](#cite_note-44)[[45]](#cite_note-45)

As one of the [suburban “WOW” counties surrounding Milwaukee](/wiki/WOW_counties "WOW counties"), Ozaukee County is a Republican stronghold in U.S. presidential elections, having voted Republican in all elections (except one) since 1940. [Lyndon B. Johnson](/wiki/Lyndon_B._Johnson "Lyndon B. Johnson") was the last Democrat to carry the county in a presidential election, in 1964. Following similar suburban trends across the nation, the county has grown more Democratic in recent years, but is still a Republican stronghold. Before WWI, however, Ozaukee County was reliably Democratic, due to its high German Catholic population.

In 2020, [Joe Biden](/wiki/Joe_Biden "Joe Biden") became the first Democrat to win over 40% of the vote since 1964.[[46]](#cite_note-Leip-46) Biden also won the municipality of [Cedarburg](/wiki/Cedarburg,_Wisconsin "Cedarburg, Wisconsin"), the first time a Democrat has won a municipality in any of the WOW Counties since 1996.[[47]](#cite_note-47) Progressive judge [Janet Protasiewicz](/wiki/Janet_Protasiewicz "Janet Protasiewicz") received nearly 48% of the vote in Ozaukee in the [2023 Wisconsin Supreme Court election](/wiki/2023_Wisconsin_Supreme_Court_election "2023 Wisconsin Supreme Court election").[[48]](#cite_note-48) Judge [Susan M. Crawford](/wiki/Susan_M._Crawford "Susan M. Crawford") was also able to win 48% in [2025](/wiki/2025_Wisconsin_Supreme_Court_election "2025 Wisconsin Supreme Court election").

In 2024, despite losing the state to [Donald Trump](/wiki/Donald_Trump "Donald Trump"), Democrat [Kamala Harris](/wiki/Kamala_Harris "Kamala Harris") won 43.92% of the vote in Ozaukee in the [2024 United States presidential election](/wiki/2024_United_States_presidential_election_in_Wisconsin "2024 United States presidential election in Wisconsin"), the highest percentage won by a Democratic presidential nominee since 1964.
